items.q: Did Weber reduce class to economics alone, like Marx?
items.correct: No - Weber added status and party (power) alongside economic class.
items.misconception: Weber's view is identical to Marx's.
items.why: Weber saw stratification as multidimensional: class, status and party, not just economic class.

items.q: What is the functionalist view of social inequality?
items.correct: That stratification is functional, rewarding important roles to motivate talent (Davis and Moore).
items.misconception: That inequality serves no purpose whatsoever.
items.random_wrong: That all jobs are and should be paid equally.
items.why: Functionalists argue unequal rewards encourage people to train for and fill key positions.

items.correct: A group seen as positioned below the working class, often long-term unemployed or marginalised.
items.why: The underclass debate (e.g. Murray) concerns those excluded from regular employment and the class system.

items.q: Goldthorpe's scheme is the basis of which UK classification?
items.why: Correct answer: NS-SEC. “RG” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The concept of an 'underclass' in 1990 came from:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Murray. “Wright” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many classes are in Goldthorpe's scheme?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 classes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
